🟡Intermediate ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. Integrations and availability across third-party tools change frequently and vary by plan/region — confirm current options with Anthropic and the tool vendor. Confirm against the official source. You don't always go to an AI — increasingly it shows up inside the apps you already work in. Meeting Claude where you are removes the copy-paste tax and fits AI into real workflows.
documents.s: AI in Your Existing Tools (M365 / Workspace / Slack)

documents.t: Productivity suites — assistants embedded in documents, email, spreadsheets, and slides (Microsoft 365, Google Workspace). Draft, summarize, and transform content without leaving the file. Chat/collaboration — assistants and bots in tools like Slack for summarizing threads, drafting replies, and answering from your knowledge base. Browsers — read and act on the current page — see Claude in Chrome & Computer Use. Your own apps — via Connectors (MCP) and the API.

Use an existing integration — install the official app/add-in/bot where it exists. Fastest path. Connect via MCP — Connectors let Claude reach external tools/data through the open Model Context Protocol, so it can work with your systems.
documents.s: Two ways to "bring AI to your tools"

documents.t: Start with read/summarize tasks (low risk) before letting an integration take actions. Mind permissions — an integration that can act on your accounts inherits real power; grant least privilege (Securing Agents). Watch for prompt injection when AI reads untrusted content (emails, docs, web). Check data terms for work data — see Privacy.
Connectors (MCP) in the Apps Claude in Chrome & Computer Use Securing Agents & Tools

🟢Beginner ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. Artifact capabilities (interactivity, persistence, what they can call) evolve quickly — confirm current behavior in the app/help center. Confirm against the official source. Artifacts are outputs Claude renders in a side panel next to the chat — a document, a chart, a working app, a diagram — that you can see, use, and iterate on, separate from the conversation text.

Mini web apps & tools — a calculator, a quiz, a form, a small interactive demo. Documents — structured write-ups you can refine and export. Visuals — charts, diagrams, and simple data dashboards. Code you can read and run.

You can build something usable — "make me a tip calculator for a group dinner," "a dashboard from this CSV" — by describing it, then refine it conversationally ("add a service-charge field," "make the buttons bigger"). It's the clearest example of building with AI without writing code yourself.
Ask for the thing, with specifics (purpose, inputs, look). Iterate in plain language — Claude updates the same artifact. Use it in the panel; export/share where supported.

Be concrete about inputs/outputs and audience — same as good prompting. Iterate small. One change at a time is easier to get right. Verify any logic/numbers an artifact computes for important uses (Hallucinations).
Generating Real Files (docx/pptx/xlsx/pdf) Getting Started with Claude.ai Data Analysis playbook

🟡Intermediate ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. Browser/computer-use availability (beta status, platforms, plans) and exact behavior change often — confirm current details and limits in the official docs. Confirm against the official source. Claude can do more than talk — it can operate software: read and act on a web page in the browser, or control a computer (move the cursor, click, type) to complete tasks across apps. Powerful, and worth understanding the guardrails.

Claude in Chrome — a browser-side agent that can read the current page and take actions in it (navigate, click, fill forms) on your behalf. Computer use — more general control of a desktop (screenshots + mouse/keyboard) to operate native apps and multi-app workflows. Use them for things that are otherwise tedious: pulling info across pages, filling repetitive forms, multi-step web tasks.

Letting an AI click and type on your behalf is exactly where prompt injection gets dangerous: a malicious web page can contain instructions that try to hijack the agent. :::warning Treat links and pages as untrusted Be present and supervise for anything consequential — don't walk away mid-task. Don't point it at sensitive accounts (banking, admin) casually. Verify destinations — an agent shouldn't follow a suspicious link or act on instructions hidden in a page. Least privilege — restricted/permissioned modes exist precisely so the agent can look but not act where it shouldn't. :::

Good fit Be careful Research across pages, read-only Anything that spends money Filling a known, benign form Logging into sensitive accounts Extracting info you supervise Acting on emails/pages from strangers
Prompt Injection Explained Securing Agents & Tools AI in Your Existing Tools

🟡Intermediate ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. Which connectors exist, and availability by plan, change frequently — confirm current options in the app/help center. Confirm against the official source. Connectors let the Claude apps reach outside the chat — into your tools and data (drives, issue trackers, calendars, databases, and more) — so Claude can answer from, and act on, real systems. Under the hood they're powered by the open Model Context Protocol (MCP).

Without connectors, Claude only knows what's in the conversation. With a connector, it can (with your permission) pull relevant info from a connected service — e.g. find a document, read recent issues, check a calendar — and use it in its answer.
Connectors are the app-facing form of MCP. The very same protocol powers MCP in Claude Code and on the API. Learn the concept once; it applies across surfaces.
Connect the service (authorize via OAuth, where supported). Grant least privilege — only the access the task needs. Ask naturally — "find my Q3 planning doc and summarize the risks."

:::warning A connector is access + (sometimes) actions Authorize only services and scopes you trust. Content pulled from external sources can carry prompt injection — be cautious when a connector reads untrusted material. Review what a third-party connector can do before enabling it (Reviewing Third-Party Code). :::
MCP Servers in Claude Code MCP & Connecting to Tools (API) AI in Your Existing Tools

🟢Beginner ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. The exact names and locations of custom instructions and styles in the Claude apps change — confirm in the app/help center. Confirm against the official source. Tired of repeating "be concise" or "I'm a nurse, explain accordingly" every chat? Custom instructions and styles let you set your defaults once and have them apply everywhere.

Set standing facts and preferences — who you are, what you do, how you like answers — and Claude applies them across conversations. It's the consumer-app version of a system prompt (and the cousin of CLAUDE.md for developers). Good things to include: Context about you ("I run a small bakery"; "I code in Python"). Output preferences ("default to short bullet answers"; "always show your reasoning"). Hard rules ("never use emoji"; "metric units").
documents.s: Custom instructions = your personal system prompt

documents.t: Styles change tone/format (concise, formal, explanatory, etc.) and can be switched per conversation. Use a style when you want a different voice for this chat without rewriting your standing instructions.

More specific/later context tends to win when there's a conflict — so a Project's instructions or an explicit ask in your message can override your global defaults. Keep them consistent to avoid surprises.
Keep instructions short and true — like CLAUDE.md, bloat and stale rules hurt. Don't put secrets in custom instructions. Revisit them occasionally as your needs change.
System, User & Assistant Roles Projects: Persistent Workspaces CLAUDE.md & Memory Files

🟢Beginner ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. Which file types Claude can generate and how varies by plan/surface and changes — confirm in the app/help center. Confirm against the official source. Beyond text on screen, Claude can produce real, downloadable files — Word documents, PowerPoint decks, Excel spreadsheets, PDFs — formatted and ready to use. Great when you need a deliverable, not just an answer.

Word (.docx) — reports, letters, proposals, formatted docs. PowerPoint (.pptx) — slide decks from an outline or notes. Excel (.xlsx) — spreadsheets with data, formulas, simple models. PDF — print-ready versions of the above.

Describe the deliverable: type, audience, length, sections, tone. Provide the content or source (notes, data, an outline) — or ask Claude to draft it first. Ask for the file ("export this as a .docx"), then iterate ("add a title page," "make the table a chart," "tighten slide 3"). Turn these notes into a 6-slide investor update deck (.pptx): traction, revenue, roadmap, team, ask. Clean and minimal. Notes: {paste}
documents.s: How to get a good file

documents.t: Give structure. Headings/sections in your request → cleaner output. Provide real data for spreadsheets — and verify any computed numbers (Hallucinations); don't ship figures you haven't checked. Refine before exporting — it's easier to fix in chat than in the final file. These capabilities are powered by Skills under the hood — the same idea you can author yourself.
Artifacts: Live, Runnable Outputs Writing & Content Creation Business, Marketing & Strategy

🟢Beginner ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-28. Plan names and limits change — see Plans, Limits & Billing and the official pricing page for current details. Confirm against the official source. claude.ai is the easiest way to use Claude — no install, no code. Here's how to get value in your first sitting.

Create an account at claude.ai. The free tier is plenty to learn the ropes; paid tiers raise usage limits and unlock more. You don't need to decide now — see Plans, Limits & Billing when you're ready.
documents.s: Sign in and pick a plan

documents.t: A message box at the bottom — type or paste here. The conversation above — your turns and Claude's, in order. A sidebar of past chats — each chat is its own context. Attachments — drop in files, images, or PDFs to ask about them. It's also on mobile and by voice.

Don't "search" — ask like you'd ask a sharp colleague, with context and a clear ask: "I'm planning a 3-day trip to Rome in October for two people who love food and history, on a mid-range budget. Draft a day-by-day outline with one must-do per day and a nearby lunch spot." Then refine in plain language: "swap day 2 for something less touristy," "add rough costs," "make it a checklist." This back-and-forth is where the magic is — see Prompting Basics.

Give context. Goal, audience, format, tone. (It can't read your mind.) Iterate. First answer is a draft. Verify what matters. Facts, numbers, and citations can be confidently wrong — see Hallucinations.
Projects — a workspace with its own files and instructions. Custom instructions & styles — set your defaults once. Artifacts — get live documents and runnable apps in a side panel.
Your First 5 Minutes Talking to Claude (Voice Mode) Everyday Productivity

🟢Beginner ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. Cross-chat memory behavior and availability vary by plan and change — confirm current details in the app/help center. Confirm against the official source. By default each conversation is a blank slate. Memory features let Claude carry useful facts about you across chats — so you don't re-introduce yourself every time.

There are a few distinct things people call "memory" — don't confuse them: Kind What it is Context window Working memory within one chat (forgotten when it ends) — see Tokens & Context Cross-chat memory Claude remembers facts about you across conversations (the topic of this page) Custom instructions Standing facts you set explicitly Projects Knowledge scoped to a workspace

When enabled, Claude can recall things like your preferences, ongoing projects, or details you've shared — making it feel more like a continuous assistant than a stranger each session. Availability and controls depend on your plan.

Review and edit what's remembered (where the app provides controls). Clear or disable it if you want a clean slate or more privacy. Don't rely on it for accuracy of important facts — confirm critical details rather than trusting recall. Mind privacy — memory persists information; don't store sensitive data you wouldn't want kept. See Privacy. :::tip Explicit beats implicit For facts that must be applied (your role, units, hard rules), set custom instructions or use a Project — that's more reliable than hoping memory recalls them. :::
Tokens, Context & Memory Custom Instructions & Styles Privacy & Data Handling

🟢Beginner ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. Mobile features and platform availability change — confirm current capabilities in the app stores / official docs. Confirm against the official source. Claude isn't just a desktop tab. The iOS and Android apps put it in your pocket — ideal for capturing ideas, quick questions, and voice on the go.

Capture on the move. A thought in the elevator, a photo of a whiteboard, a quick "summarize this" — mobile shines for input you'd lose otherwise. Voice-first. Talking is often faster than typing on a phone — see Voice Mode. Photos & screenshots. Snap a menu, a form, an error message, a sign, and ask about it (multimodal input). Continuity. Conversations and Projects sync, so you can start on mobile and finish on desktop.
Long-form writing and editing. Working with big documents or lots of files. Artifacts and anything where screen space helps. Developer work (Claude Code is desktop/CLI).

Capture by voice → refine on screen. Brain-dump a messy idea by voice while walking, then later ask Claude to "turn that transcript into a clean outline." The phone gathers raw material; you polish anywhere. :::tip Use the share sheet On mobile you can often share text/links/images straight into Claude from other apps — a fast way to ask "summarize this" without copy-paste. :::
Talking to Claude (Voice Mode) Getting Started with Claude.ai Everyday Productivity

🟢Beginner ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-28. Plan names, prices, and limits change regularly — always check the official pricing page for current numbers. We deliberately don't quote dollar amounts here. Confirm against the official source. Which Claude plan do you need? Here's how to think about it — without quoting prices that would be stale next month (check the official pricing for those).

Tier Roughly for Free Trying Claude, light personal use Pro Regular individual use — more usage, more capability Max Heavy individual users who hit Pro limits Team Small teams — shared workspace and management Enterprise Orgs needing admin, security, scale, and controls (Exact names/structure can change — confirm on the pricing page.)

Consumer plans cap how much you can use in a window (messages/usage that reset over time), and higher tiers raise those caps. When you hit a limit you're typically asked to wait until it resets or upgrade — your work isn't lost, you just pause. Heavier models (and longer conversations) consume your allowance faster. If you're hitting limits, a quick fix is to start fresh chats for new topics and keep prompts focused (same idea as context management).

Start Free. Learn what you actually use it for. Upgrade to Pro when limits interrupt real work. Go Max if you're a power user still hitting caps. Team/Enterprise when colleagues need shared access, admin, or compliance.
App plans = a flat subscription with usage limits (this page). API billing = pay per token for what you build. Different model, different page.
documents.s: Apps vs API billing — different things
