Not sure which fits? Start with Your First 5 Minutes — it's useful no matter where you end up. And read How Levels & Freshness Work so the badges and dates make sense.

⚪All levels Two small conventions make AILmanac easy to navigate and easy to trust. Master them once, and every page becomes faster to read and easier to rely on. 🎯What you'll learn ✅Read the four level badges and know which pages are written for you ✅Spot a freshness stamp and interpret an old date correctly ✅Tell evergreen pages from volatile ones at a glance ✅Know where every model fact lives — the single source of truth

Every page starts with a badge telling you who it's for. Here are all four, exactly as they appear: 🟢Beginner No prior knowledge assumed. Start here if you're new. 🟡Intermediate You've used AI a bit and want to go deeper. 🔴Advanced Building things — agents, the API, custom setups. ⚪All levels Useful at any level. Badges rely on text, not just color, so they work for everyone (including screen readers and color-blind readers). Skip what's above your level — or stretch into it when you're ready. ✨Pro tip →Want the pages strung together in a sensible order for your reader type? Follow a Learning Path. The Learning Paths string pages together in a sensible order per reader type.

AI moves fast. Anything that changes — model names, prices, limits, UI labels, beta features — is a volatile fact, and on AILmanac those carry a stamp like this: ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. This is what a freshness stamp looks like. The date is when a human last checked it; the link is the official source that always wins. Confirm against the official source. How to read what you see: Evergreen pages (concepts like "what is an LLM") don't carry a date — they don't go stale, so a date would be noise. Volatile pages show the date so you know how current the specifics are. An old date isn't a bug — it's honesty. It tells you to double-check that particular fact rather than assume. ⚠️Watch out •When the date looks old, don't treat the specific number or label as gospel — open the linked official source and confirm. The source always wins over the page.

Guided walkthrough1 of 4 1Read the badge first⌄ Glance at the badge under the title. If it's above your level, skip it for now or stretch in when ready; if it matches you, dive in. ← PreviousNext → 2Check for a date stamp⌄ No stamp means the page is evergreen and won't go stale. A stamp means the page carries volatile facts. ← PreviousNext → 3Judge the date⌄ A recent date means the specifics are current. An old date is a signal to verify, not a sign the page is broken. ← PreviousNext → 4Follow the source link when it matters⌄ For anything you'll act on — a price, a limit, a model name — open the official source linked in the stamp. It always wins. ← PreviousNext →
documents.s: How to use both conventions on any page

documents.t: Model identities live in one place — the Current Models & Pricing table — kept verified and dated. No model fact is hard-coded elsewhere; everything links there. So there's a single source of truth to trust.

A guide that's confidently out of date is worse than no guide. These conventions let us be complete without pretending nothing changes — and let you calibrate how much to trust any given line. Lock it in tap to flip ✨TermBeginner badgeDefinitionNo prior knowledge assumed. Start here if you're new.Term shown. ←Prev 1 / 8 Next→ 🧠 Check yourself 0/3 1A page shows a 'last verified' date from several months ago. What does that mean? The page is broken and should be ignoredIt's honesty — a signal to double-check that fact rather than assumeThe page is evergreen and never changes 2Why do badges use text and not just color? To save on design effortSo they work for everyone, including screen readers and color-blind readersBecause color is reserved for the freshness stamp 3Where do model identities live? Hard-coded on each page that mentions themIn one place — the Current Models & Pricing table — with everything else linking thereOnly in the official Anthropic docs 💡Key takeaways ⭐Four badges (beginner, intermediate, advanced, all) tell you who a page is for — and they use text, not just color. ⭐Volatile facts carry a 'last verified' date plus an official source link that always wins; evergreen pages carry no date. ⭐An old date is honesty, not a bug — verify, don't assume. ⭐All model identities live in one verified, dated source of truth: the Current Models & Pricing table.
Welcome & Why This Exists The Landscape Map Current Models & Pricing

⚪All levels AILmanac is a free, community-built almanac for getting the most out of Claude — and any AI. An almanac is a reference you come back to: complete, plain-spoken, and kept current. That's the goal here. 🎯What you'll learn ✅Understand what AILmanac is — and what it deliberately is not ✅Find the level that fits you: beginner, intermediate, or advanced ✅Pick your fastest way in and start reading in under a minute

Everyone, on purpose — and we tell you the level of every page so you never feel lost or talked down to: Level This is you if... 🟢Beginner You've barely used AI and want a guaranteed first win. 🟡Intermediate You use Claude already and want noticeably better results. 🔴Advanced You build with Claude Code, the API, agents, and MCP.

There are excellent resources already (see the Landscape Map). AILmanac is not a link dump and not a copy of the official docs. It is: Opinionated — we tell you the one recommended way, then the alternatives. Level-tagged — every page is badged so you read what's right for you. Template-rich — copy-paste starters you can use in 30 seconds. Honest about freshness — facts that change (model names, prices, features) carry a "last verified" date and a link to the source.

:::warning The official docs are the source of truth When AILmanac and the official Anthropic documentation disagree, the official docs win. AI tools change fast; we work hard to stay current, but always confirm anything critical against the source we link to. ::: We also don't store your data, can't see your account, and won't make decisions for you on high-stakes work — see Responsible Use.

Pick the door that fits where you are right now: Guided walkthrough1 of 3 1Brand new?⌄ Start with Your First 5 Minutes for a guaranteed first win — /docs/start-here/your-first-5-minutes. ← PreviousNext → 2Want a plan?⌄ Choose a Learning Path to follow a structured route — /docs/start-here/learning-paths. ← PreviousNext → 3Confused by a term?⌄ Jump to the Glossary for plain-spoken definitions — /docs/start-here/glossary. ← PreviousNext → Brand new? → Your First 5 Minutes Want a plan? → Choose a Learning Path Confused by a term? → Glossary

AILmanac stays complete because people add to it. Found a gap, an error, or something newly shipped? Contributing takes 10 minutes. 🧠 Check yourself 0/3 1When AILmanac and the official Anthropic docs disagree, which one wins? AILmanac, because it's opinionatedThe official Anthropic docsWhichever was updated first 2You're brand new to AI. Which door should you take first? Your First 5 MinutesChoose a Learning PathThe Glossary 3What does AILmanac do with your data and account? It stores your data to personalize pagesIt can see your account to give adviceIt stores nothing and can't see your account 💡Key takeaways ⭐AILmanac is a free, community-built, opinionated, level-tagged reference for getting the most out of Claude and any AI ⭐Every page is badged beginner, intermediate, or advanced so you read what fits you ⭐The official Anthropic docs are always the source of truth ⭐Three ways in: Your First 5 Minutes, a Learning Path, or the Glossary Next: Your First 5 Minutes · Choose a Learning Path · Glossary

🟢Beginner "Claude" comes in a few flavors. Pick by what you're trying to do, not by which you've heard of. 🎯What you'll learn ✅Match your goal to the right Claude surface: chat, Claude Code, or the API ✅Know when mobile and voice fit into the picture ✅Understand how the three surfaces work together as you level up ✅Get a quick read on which model to reach for once you start building

Surface Best for Who Start here Claude.ai Writing, research, analysis, learning, planning, everyday questions Everyone, no setup Getting Started with Claude.ai Claude Code Working in a codebase — reading, editing, running commands, fixing tests Developers (and the technically curious) What Claude Code Is API & Agent SDK Apps, automations, and agents that call Claude programmatically Developers shipping a product or pipeline Your First API Call
documents.s: The three surfaces at a glance

documents.t: Claude.ai is the no-setup starting point for everyone. You also get it on mobile (iOS/Android) and by voice — great for capturing ideas on the go. Power it up with Projects, custom instructions, and Artifacts.
Claude Code works inside your project. It reads, edits, runs commands, and fixes tests — acting on your files with your permission.
documents.s: Claude Code — the agentic coding tool

documents.t: The API and Agent SDK let your own software call Claude programmatically, so you can ship AI features, automations, and agents.
documents.s: The API & Agent SDK — build Claude into your own software

documents.t: These aren't rival products — most people graduate across them: You want to… Use Draft an email, summarize a PDF, brainstorm Claude.ai (or voice/mobile) Refactor a module, add tests, fix a bug Claude Code Add an AI feature to your app The API / Agent SDK :::tip Not sure? Start with chat Claude.ai needs zero setup and teaches you how Claude "thinks." The skills transfer everywhere else. :::

Picking a surface is step one. When you move to Claude Code or the API, you also pick a model — Haiku, Sonnet, or Opus. Answer three quick questions and this picker suggests a starting point: Task difficulty SimpleMediumHard What matters most QualityBalancedCost / speed Request volume LowHigh Claude Sonnet The balanced default — strong reasoning and coding at a fraction of Opus cost. Start here, and only move up if you hit a real quality ceiling. Look up the exact model ID on the models table. Rule of thumb only — run a quick eval on your own inputs to be sure. :::note Don't hard-code the names Model lineups and prices change. Always confirm the current model IDs on the Choosing a Claude Model page before you ship. :::

🧠 Check yourself 0/3 1You want to draft an email and summarize a PDF — no setup. Which surface? Claude CodeClaude.ai (chat / mobile / voice)The API & Agent SDK 2You need to refactor a module and fix failing tests inside a real project. Which surface? Claude.aiClaude CodeThe API & Agent SDK 3Where should you confirm the current model names and prices? This pageThe Choosing a Claude Model pageThe Mermaid diagram above 💡Key takeaways ⭐Claude.ai: zero-setup chat for writing, research, and everyday work — also on mobile and by voice ⭐Claude Code: an agent that acts inside your codebase ⭐API & Agent SDK: build Claude into your own software ⭐They compose — most people start with chat and graduate to Code and the API ⭐Pick a model (Haiku / Sonnet / Opus) only once you're building, and verify current IDs before shipping
Your First 5 Minutes Learning Paths Choosing a Claude Model (once you're building)

🟢Beginner No setup, no jargon. In five minutes you'll get one genuinely useful result and understand why it worked. 🎯What you'll learn ✅Open Claude and ask your first real question (no setup needed) ✅Write a prompt the way a sharp colleague would want it: goal, length, audience, tone ✅Refine an answer with plain-language follow-ups (iterative refinement) ✅Adopt the one safety habit that matters: verify before you trust :::tip Add a screenshot here This page is the beginner floor — it deserves annotated screenshots or a short GIF. If you can contribute visuals, please open a PR. :::

Guided walkthrough1 of 5 1Minute 1 — Open Claude⌄ Go to claude.ai and sign in (the free tier is enough to start). You can also use Claude on the mobile app or by voice — see Which Claude Should I Use? for the full map. ← PreviousNext → 2Minute 2 — Ask like you'd ask a sharp colleague⌄ Don't 'search.' Give context and say what you want: name the goal, length, audience, tone, and what 'done' looks like. That's 80% of prompting, and it works on every AI. ← PreviousNext → 3Minute 3 — Read, then refine⌄ You don't have to get it perfect first try. Reply in plain language — 'make it shorter and more casual,' 'add a sentence about the free Q&A.' This back-and-forth is iterative refinement. ← PreviousNext → 4Minute 4 — Try a second, different task⌄ Prove to yourself it's general-purpose: summarize an article, explain a concept simply, or plan a quick dinner from the ingredients you have. ← PreviousNext → 5Minute 5 — Learn the one safety habit⌄ AI can sound confident and still be wrong. For anything that matters, check it. Verify facts, numbers, dates, quotes, and citations. ← PreviousNext →

The biggest first-day win: give context and say what you want. Compare: ❌ "marketing email" ✅ "Write a friendly 120-word email inviting existing customers to a webinar on June 30 about our new analytics dashboard. Warm but not salesy. End with a clear RSVP call to action." The second prompt names the goal, length, audience, tone, and what 'done' looks like. That's 80% of prompting, and it works on every AI. (More in Prompting Basics.) ✦ Your first prompt to try CopyOpen in Claude ↗ Write a friendly 120-word email inviting existing customers to a webinar on June 30 about our new analytics dashboard. Warm but not salesy. End with a clear RSVP call to action.
documents.s: Minute 2, up close — the single biggest beginner upgrade

documents.t: You don't have to get it perfect first try. Just reply like you would to a colleague: "Make it shorter and more casual." "Add a sentence about the free Q&A at the end." "Give me 3 subject-line options." This back-and-forth — iterative refinement — beats agonizing over one perfect prompt.
documents.s: Minute 3, up close — refine in plain language

documents.t: Pick one to prove it's general-purpose: "Summarise this article in 5 bullet points: [paste text]." "Explain compound interest to me like I'm 12, with one example." "I have chicken, rice, and spinach. Give me a 20-minute dinner."
documents.s: Minute 4, up close — try a second, different task

documents.t: :::warning Verify before you trust AI can sound confident and still be wrong — especially about facts, numbers, dates, quotes, and citations. For anything that matters, check it. This isn't a flaw you can prompt away; it's how the tool works. See Hallucinations & How to Reduce Them. ::: 💡Key takeaways ⭐Context beats keywords: state the goal, length, audience, tone, and what 'done' looks like ⭐Iterative refinement (plain-language follow-ups) beats chasing one perfect prompt ⭐Claude is general-purpose — try a second, unrelated task to feel it ⭐Always verify anything that matters; confidence is not correctness
documents.s: Minute 5, up close — the one safety habit

documents.t: 🧠 Check yourself 0/3 1What is the single biggest beginner upgrade to your prompts? Using more keywords, like a search engineGiving context and saying what you want (goal, length, audience, tone)Always paying for the premium tier first 2Your first answer isn't quite right. What does the page recommend? Start over and engineer one perfect promptReply in plain language to refine it (iterative refinement)Accept it as-is because you can't change it 3What is the one safety habit to adopt from minute 5? Trust confident-sounding answersVerify anything that matters — facts, numbers, dates, quotes, citationsOnly ask questions you already know the answer to

Where to next: Make every answer better → Prompting Basics Pick a structured plan → Learning Paths Use AI for real work → Everyday Productivity Something felt off? → Troubleshooting: "Why did Claude do that?"

🟡Intermediate ✅ Last verified: 2026-07-01. SDK method names and parameters evolve — confirm against the official API reference. Model IDs: see the models table. Confirm against the official source. Battle-tested shapes for building on the API. Adapt and harden for your stack.

Python TypeScript import time, random, anthropic client = anthropic.Anthropic() def ask(messages, model="claude-sonnet-5", max_tokens=1024, system=None): for attempt in range(5): try: return client.messages.create( model=model, max_tokens=max_tokens, system=system or anthropic.NOT_GIVEN, messages=messages, ) except (anthropic.RateLimitError, anthropic.APIStatusError) as e: if attempt == 4: raise time.sleep(min(2 ** attempt + random.random(), 30)) import Anthropic from "@anthropic-ai/sdk"; const client = new Anthropic(); export async function ask(messages, { model = "claude-sonnet-5", maxTokens = 1024, system } = {}) { for (let attempt = 0; attempt < 5; attempt++) { try { return await client.messages.create({ model, max_tokens: maxTokens, system, messages }); } catch (e: any) { if (attempt === 4 || ![429, 500, 529].includes(e?.status)) throw e; await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, Math.min(2 ** attempt * 1000 + Math.random() * 1000, 30000))); } } } (SDKs also retry transient errors by default — know your client's behavior before stacking your own. See Errors & Rate Limits.)
with client.messages.stream(model="claude-sonnet-5", max_tokens=1024, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}]) as stream: for text in stream.text_stream: print(text, end="", flush=True)

messages = [{"role": "user", "content": "What's the weather in Rome?"}] while True: resp = client.messages.create(model="claude-sonnet-5", max_tokens=1024, tools=TOOLS, messages=messages) if resp.stop_reason != "tool_use": break messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content}) results = [run_tool(b) for b in resp.content if b.type == "tool_use"] messages.append({"role": "user", "content": results}) # tool_result blocks :::warning Hardening Cap iterations and cost, validate tool inputs, and keep secrets in env vars. For autonomous use, read Securing Agents. :::
Your First API Call · Tool Use Building Agents on the API Errors, Rate Limits & Reliability

⚪All levels CLAUDE.md is the highest-leverage thing you can give Claude Code: persistent instructions it reads every session. Drop one of these at your repo root (or run /init to generate a starting point), then trim it to fit. Use the copy button on each block. :::tip Keep it short and true A CLAUDE.md that's long, aspirational, or out of date hurts — Claude follows it literally. Describe how the project actually works, and prune ruthlessly. Details on the CLAUDE.md page. ::: Jump to your stack: Universal · Node / TypeScript · Python / FastAPI · Django · Go · Rust

# Project: <name> ## What this is <One or two sentences: what the project does and who uses it.> ## Tech stack - Language/runtime: <e.g. TypeScript, Node 20> - Framework: <e.g. Next.js 14 App Router> - Key libraries: <e.g. Prisma, tRPC, Tailwind> ## How to run - Install: `<cmd>` - Dev server: `<cmd>` - Tests: `<cmd>` - Lint/format: `<cmd>` ## Conventions - <e.g. Use functional components; no class components.> - <e.g. Co-locate tests as *.test.ts next to source.> - <e.g. Conventional Commits for messages.> ## Guardrails - Run the tests before saying a task is done. - Don't edit files under `/generated` or `/vendor`. - Never commit secrets or .env files. ## Good to know - <Gotchas, non-obvious decisions, links to deeper docs.>

# Project: <name> (Next.js + TypeScript) ## How to run - Dev: `npm run dev` - Build: `npm run build` - Test: `npm test` - Lint: `npm run lint` (must pass before commit) ## Conventions - TypeScript strict; no `any` without a comment justifying it. - Components in `src/components`, one folder per component. - Data fetching via tRPC; never call the DB from a client component. - Styling: Tailwind utility classes; no inline styles. ## Guardrails - After any change, run `npm run lint && npm test`. - Keep files under ~300 lines; split when larger. - Do not modify `prisma/migrations` by hand.

# Project: <name> (FastAPI) ## How to run - Install: `uv sync` (or `pip install -e .`) - Dev: `uvicorn app.main:app --reload` - Test: `pytest` - Lint/format: `ruff check . && ruff format .` ## Conventions - Type hints everywhere; prefer `pathlib` over `os.path`. - Pydantic models for all request/response bodies. - f-strings, not %-formatting. ## Guardrails - Run `pytest` and `ruff check` before completing a task. - Fail fast with descriptive errors; no silent excepts.

# Project: <name> (Django <version>) ## Tech stack - Python 3.12 · Django 5.x - Database: PostgreSQL (psycopg3) # or SQLite for local dev; state the truth - Task queue: Celery + Redis # remove if unused - Auth: django-allauth / dj-rest-auth # remove if unused - API layer: Django REST Framework (DRF) # remove if unused; note if it's pure server-side templates instead - Frontend: HTMX + Alpine.js / React SPA # pick one; state the truth - Key packages: django-environ, Pillow, whitenoise ## Settings layout # config/ # settings/ # base.py ← shared # local.py ← DEBUG=True, console email backend # production.py← env-driven secrets, ALLOWED_HOSTS, SECURE_* flags # Load via DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE env var. ## How to run - Install: `pip install -e ".[dev]"` or `uv sync` - Env file: copy `.env.example` → `.env` and fill secrets - Migrate: `python manage.py migrate` - Dev server: `python manage.py runserver` - Shell: `python manage.py shell_plus` # django-extensions - Test: `pytest` (pytest-django; see pytest.ini) - Lint/format: `ruff check . && ruff format .` - Type check: `mypy .` # if mypy is configured ## Project layout <project_slug>/ <app_name>/ models.py # one model family per app; no god-models views.py # CBVs preferred; FBVs for simple one-offs serializers.py # DRF only urls.py # app-level URL conf; included from config/urls.py services.py # business logic lives here, NOT in views or models tasks.py # Celery tasks tests/ test_models.py test_views.py config/ settings/ urls.py wsgi.py / asgi.py manage.py ## Conventions ### Models - Fat service layer, thin models: domain logic goes in `services.py`, not model methods. - Always set `verbose_name` and `verbose_name_plural` on Meta. - Use `get_object_or_404` or `select_related`/`prefetch_related` — never N+1 queries. - UUID primary keys for all user-facing models: `id = models.UUIDField(primary_key=True, default=uuid.uuid4, editable=False)`. ### Migrations - One migration per logical change; never squash without team sign-off. - Never edit a migration that has been applied to staging or production. - Name meaningful ones: `python manage.py makemigrations --name add_published_at_to_post`. ### Views & URLs - Class-Based Views for CRUD and list/detail; Function-Based Views for anything with unusual branching. - DRF ViewSets for API endpoints; routers in `<app>/urls.py`. - All URLs namespaced: `app_name = "blog"` in each app's `urls.py`. ### Templates (if server-side rendered) - Extend `base.html`; block names: `title`, `content`, `extra_css`, `extra_js`. - No business logic in templates; use template tags or pass computed context from the view. ### Security - `SECRET_KEY` and all credentials via environment variables (django-environ); never hardcoded. - CSRF on all mutating endpoints; DRF uses `SessionAuthentication` + CSRF by default. - `SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT`, `SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE`, `CSRF_COOKIE_SECURE` = True in production. ## Guardrails - Run `pytest && ruff check .` before marking any task done. - Never edit applied migrations; create a new one instead. - Do not add raw SQL unless there is no ORM equivalent — document why if you do. - Do not import from `django.conf.settings` inside model files; pass values explicitly. - After adding a new dependency, update `pyproject.toml` (or `requirements/*.txt`) and note it here. ## Good to know - `DEBUG=True` in local; `DEBUG=False` in all server environments — never skip this. - Static files are served by WhiteNoise in production; run `collectstatic` before deploy. - Celery worker: `celery -A config worker -l info`; beat: `celery -A config beat -l info`. - <Add any project-specific gotchas: custom user model, multi-tenancy, third-party integrations.>

# Project: <name> (Go <version>) ## What this is <One or two sentences: what the service does and who uses it.> ## Tech stack - Go 1.23+ - HTTP: net/http stdlib / chi / gin # pick one; delete the rest - Database: pgx + sqlc # or database/sql + sqlx; state what's true - Migrations: golang-migrate # remove if unused - Config: envconfig / godotenv # remove if unused - Key deps: zap (logging), testify (assertions) ## How to run - Install deps: `go mod download` - Build: `go build ./...` - Dev (live): `air` (github.com/air-verse/air) # remove if unused - Test: `go test ./... -race` - Lint: `golangci-lint run` - Generate: `go generate ./...` # sqlc, mockgen, etc. ## Project layout cmd/ <service>/ main.go # wiring only — no business logic internal/ <domain>/ service.go # business logic repository.go # DB layer (interface + impl) handler.go # HTTP handlers pkg/ # code safe to import from outside this module migrations/ # SQL migration files ## Conventions - Package names: short, lowercase, no underscores (`userstore` not `user_store`). - One package per domain concern inside `internal/`; avoid a god `utils` package. - Errors: always wrap with `fmt.Errorf("doing X: %w", err)`; never discard. - Interfaces: define them in the *consumer* package, not the producer. - No global state; inject dependencies via constructor functions. - context.Context is the first parameter of every function that does I/O. - Prefer table-driven tests; subtests via `t.Run`. ## Guardrails - Run `go test ./... -race && golangci-lint run` before marking any task done. - Never use `panic` in library code; return errors. - Do not use `init()` functions — wire everything in `main`. - Avoid naked `interface{}`; use typed interfaces or generics (Go 1.18+). - Keep `main.go` thin: parse config, build deps, call `server.Run`. ## Good to know - `go generate` must be run after changing any `.sql` query files (sqlc). - Linter config lives in `.golangci.yml` — do not add lint exceptions without a comment. - <Add project-specific gotchas: service mesh, internal auth, proto generation.>

# Project: <name> (Rust <edition>) ## What this is <One or two sentences: what this binary/library does and who uses it.> ## Tech stack - Rust edition 2021, MSRV <version> - Async runtime: Tokio # or async-std; pick one - HTTP: Axum / Actix-web # remove unused - Serialisation: serde + serde_json - Database: sqlx (async, compile-time checked queries) # remove if unused - Error handling: thiserror (library) / anyhow (binary) # pick per crate type - Key crates: tracing, tracing-subscriber, dotenvy ## How to run - Build (dev): `cargo build` - Build (rel): `cargo build --release` - Test: `cargo test` - Lint: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` - Format: `cargo fmt --check` - Docs: `cargo doc --open` - Watch: `cargo watch -x test` # remove if unused ## Workspace layout (if multi-crate) Cargo.toml # [workspace] root crates/ core/ # domain logic, no I/O, no async api/ # Axum HTTP layer db/ # sqlx repository layer cli/ # binary entry-point ## Conventions - No `unwrap()` or `expect()` in library crates; propagate with `?`. - `expect()` in binaries only where a panic is truly unrecoverable — with a message explaining why. - Prefer `thiserror` for library error enums; `anyhow` for binary/main error chains. - Async only at the edges (HTTP handlers, DB calls); keep domain logic sync. - Derive `Debug`, `Clone`, `serde::Serialize/Deserialize` where it makes sense. - Feature flags in `Cargo.toml` for optional integrations; document each one. ## Guardrails - Run `cargo test && cargo clippy -- -D warnings && cargo fmt --check` before done. - Never silence a Clippy lint without a `#[allow(...)]` comment explaining why. - Do not use `unsafe` without a `// SAFETY:` comment justifying it. - Do not add a crate that duplicates one already in the dependency tree without discussion. - Keep compile times in mind: prefer concrete types over heavy generics in hot paths. ## Good to know - `sqlx` queries are checked at compile time — run `cargo sqlx prepare` to update the offline cache after changing queries. - MSRV is enforced in CI; do not use features newer than the stated MSRV. - <Add project-specific gotchas: WASM target, FFI boundaries, no_std constraints.>

Layer it. A repo-root CLAUDE.md for the whole project; nested CLAUDE.md files in subfolders for area-specific rules. Use @imports to pull in existing docs instead of duplicating them (see the CLAUDE.md reference). Review it monthly. Stale instructions are worse than none.
documents.s: Tips that apply to all of them

documents.t: The full how-to → CLAUDE.md & Memory Files More starters → SKILL.md Templates, hooks & settings recipes Improve these → Contribute

🟡Intermediate ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. Settings/hook keys evolve — confirm the current schema in the official Claude Code settings & hooks docs. Confirm against the official source. Drop-in recipes for settings.json, permissions, and hooks. Adapt commands to your stack.

.claude/settings.json (committed, team-wide): { "permissions": { "allow": [ "Read", "Bash(npm run test:*)", "Bash(npm run lint)", "Bash(git status)", "Bash(git diff:*)" ], "ask": ["Bash(npm install:*)", "Write"], "deny": ["Read(./.env)", "Read(./**/*.pem)", "Bash(git push --force:*)"] } } Put personal/machine-specific overrides in .claude/settings.local.json (git-ignored).
{ "hooks": { "PostToolUse": [ { "matcher": "Edit|Write", "hooks": [ { "type": "command", "command": "npx prettier --write \"$CLAUDE_FILE_PATH\" 2>/dev/null || true" } ] } ] } }
Block "done" until tests pass (conceptual — wire to a Stop/pre-finish event per the docs): { "hooks": { "Stop": [ { "hooks": [ { "type": "command", "command": "npm test --silent" } ] } ] } }

{ "hooks": { "PreToolUse": [ { "matcher": "Edit|Write", "hooks": [ { "type": "command", "command": "case \"$CLAUDE_FILE_PATH\" in *secrets*|*.env|*/vendor/*) echo 'blocked: protected path'; exit 1;; esac" } ] } ] } } :::tip Keep hooks fast and idempotent They run constantly. Make them quick, safe to re-run, and loud only on real problems. :::
Permissions & Permission Modes Hooks: Deterministic Automation Slash Command Library

🟡Intermediate ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. MCP config keys and SDK APIs evolve — confirm against the official MCP docs and the Claude Code MCP docs. Confirm against the official source. Copy-paste starters for connecting Claude to tools via MCP. Trim to what you need.

{ "mcpServers": { "github": { "command": "npx", "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"], "env": { "GITHUB_TOKEN": "${GITHUB_TOKEN}" } }, "postgres": { "command": "npx", "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-postgres", "${DATABASE_URL}"] } } } :::warning Keep secrets out of the file Reference env vars (${GITHUB_TOKEN}) — don't hard-code tokens in a committed file. :::

A tiny server exposing one tool. Adapt the handler to your data. import { McpServer } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js"; import { StdioServerTransport } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/stdio.js"; import { z } from "zod"; const server = new McpServer({ name: "echo", version: "1.0.0" }); server.tool( "echo", { text: z.string().describe("Text to echo back") }, async ({ text }) => ({ content: [{ type: "text", text: `You said: ${text}` }] }), ); await server.connect(new StdioServerTransport());

from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP mcp = FastMCP("echo") @mcp.tool() def echo(text: str) -> str: """Echo the text back.""" return f"You said: {text}" if __name__ == "__main__": mcp.run() # stdio transport
Least privilege — only the data/actions it needs (Securing Agents). Validate inputs; return errors as results, don't crash. Review third-party servers before connecting (Reviewing Third-Party Code).

⚪All levels Grab-and-go prompts for common tasks. They use XML tags for clean structure and bake in the habits from Prompting Basics. Fill the {braces}.

<instructions> Summarize the document for {audience} in {N} bullet points. Use ONLY the document; if something isn't there, say "not stated." Then list any action items. </instructions> <document> {paste} </document> Expected: tight bullets + action items. Common failure: invents facts → the "ONLY the document" clause guards against it.

Extract the following from the text as JSON: {field1, field2, field3}. Return ONLY valid JSON, no prose. If a field is missing, use null. <text>{paste}</text> Pair with structured output for reliability.
Rewrite the text to be {clearer/shorter/more formal} without changing meaning. Then list the changes you made as bullets. <text>{paste}</text>
Review this diff for correctness bugs, missing edge cases, and security issues. Report ONLY high-confidence findings as a checklist with file:line. Diff: <diff>{paste}</diff>
I'm deciding between {A} and {B} for {situation}. Build a trade-off table across the criteria that matter, then recommend one and name the biggest risk.
Here's my prompt: "{prompt}". Ask me the 3 questions that would most improve it, then rewrite it with sensible defaults.

You are {role}. {task}. Constraints: {format, length, tone}. If you're unsure or lack info, say so rather than guessing. :::tip Make them yours Save the ones you use as slash commands or a Project so you never retype them. :::
Prompt Patterns Library Structuring Prompts with XML Tags Production API Snippets

⚪All levels ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. Skill file layout and where skills can be used (Claude Code, Claude.ai, Cowork) evolve — confirm specifics in the official Skills docs. Confirm against the official source. A Skill gives Claude focused expertise it loads on demand when your request matches — so you don't re-explain your needs every time. AILmanac ships a set of ready-to-use skill packs, one per kind of user. Learn the concept in depth on the Skills page.

Grab any of these from the repo's /skills folder: Pack For What it does writing-content-pro Writers, marketers, creators Brief → draft in passes → match your voice → repurpose developer-workflow Developers Context-first coding, reviews, tests, clear commits research-analyst Researchers, analysts Source-grounded synthesis with citation discipline data-analyst Data & spreadsheet users SQL/formulas + plain-language reads + stat sanity checks business-marketing Founders, SMB owners Positioning, copy, customer comms, light planning student-learning Students & learners Socratic tutoring + teach-back (learn, don't just copy) everyday-productivity Everyone Email, summaries, planning, decisions Each bakes in the house rules: give context, iterate, and verify what matters.

# personal — available in all your projects mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills && cp -r skills/writing-content-pro ~/.claude/skills/ # or per-project — shareable with your team via the repo mkdir -p .claude/skills && cp -r skills/writing-content-pro .claude/skills/ Claude activates the skill automatically when your request matches its description. On Claude.ai / Cowork, create a skill and paste the pack's SKILL.md (availability varies by plan).

A skill is a folder with a SKILL.md: YAML frontmatter + instructions. The description is the trigger — write it as "Use when…". --- name: my-skill description: Use when the user is doing X — be specific about the trigger so Claude knows when to load this and when not to. --- # My Skill Short statement of the role/goal. ## Workflow 1. Step one… 2. Step two… ## Rules - The non-negotiables (accuracy, privacy, output format). ## Default output What a good response looks like.
documents.s: Write your own — the minimal template

documents.t: name is kebab-case and matches the folder. description says when to use it (the trigger), not just what it is. Instructions are specific and bounded — one clear job. No secrets, credentials, or personal/client data. States how to handle uncertainty (don't fabricate; verify).
Skills: On-Demand Expertise — the full concept Custom Slash Commands — you trigger these on demand Subagents — delegate whole chunks of work
🟡Intermediate Copy these into .claude/commands/<name>.md (project) or ~/.claude/commands/<name>.md (personal). The filename becomes the command. See Custom Slash Commands for the mechanics.

--- description: Write a Conventional Commit message from the staged changes --- Run `git diff --cached`. Write ONE Conventional Commit message (feat/fix/docs/ refactor/test/chore) describing the change. Output only the message.

--- description: Review code for bugs, edge cases, and clarity --- Review the target for correctness bugs, missing edge cases, and clarity issues. Report only real, high-confidence findings as a checklist. Target: $ARGUMENTS

--- description: Draft a pull request description from the branch diff --- Compare against the default branch (`git diff main...HEAD`). Write a PR description: what changed, why, and how it was verified. Use short sections.
--- description: Explain how something works --- Explain $ARGUMENTS in plain language: what it does, how it flows, and any gotchas. Read the relevant files first.

--- description: Scaffold a new component following project conventions --- Create a new $ARGUMENTS following this repo's existing patterns (structure, naming, tests). Show a plan first, then create the files.

--- description: Summarize recent commits into a changelog entry --- Run `git log --oneline -20`. Group the changes into Added / Changed / Fixed in Keep a Changelog style. :::tip Share them as a plugin Bundle your team's favorite commands (plus hooks and skills) into a plugin so everyone installs them in one step. :::
Custom Slash Commands Hooks & settings.json Recipes SKILL.md Templates

⚪All levels CLAUDE.md is the highest-leverage thing you can give Claude Code — persistent instructions it reads every session. Pick your stack to start from sensible, opinionated defaults, then tweak the fields and copy (or download) a ready-to-use file. Everything runs in your browser; nothing is sent anywhere. Start from a stack:Next.js + TSPython + FastAPIDjangoExpo / RNNode APIGoRustStatic site Project nameTech stackFramework (optional)Install commandDev commandTest commandLint / format What this isConventions (one per line) GuardrailsRun tests before declaring a task doneNever commit secrets / .env filesStay within the requested scopePrefer editing existing files over new onesAsk before adding new dependenciesDon't add comments unless askedDon't edit generated/vendor codeKeep files small (split > ~300 lines)Lint/format must pass before commitUse Conventional CommitsPlan before large/risky changes CLAUDE.md · 16 lines CopyDownload # Project: <name> ## What this is <One or two sentences: what the project does and who uses it.> ## Tech stack - Language / runtime: <e.g. TypeScript, Node 20> ## How to run - Install: `<cmd>` ## Guardrails - Run the tests before saying a task is complete. - Never commit secrets, API keys, or .env files. - Stay within the requested scope; flag related issues instead of fixing them unprompted. :::tip Keep it short and true Trim anything that isn't actually true of your project — Claude follows CLAUDE.md literally. See the full guide on the CLAUDE.md page. :::
CLAUDE.md & Memory Files — the full how-to CLAUDE.md Starters — hand-written templates by stack MCP Config Builder

🟢Beginner Build a clean, well-structured prompt step by step — role, context, task, examples, and output format — using the patterns Claude responds to best. Copy the result when you're done. All client-side. Role (optional)GoalContextFormatTone Allow “I don't know” (reduces made-up answers) Your promptCopy Task: <what you want done> If you're unsure or lack the information, say so instead of guessing. :::tip Structure beats cleverness A clear role, an explicit task, and a couple of good examples beat one long clever paragraph. See Prompting Basics and the Pattern Library. :::
Prompting Basics — the fundamentals Pattern Library — reusable building blocks Prompt Doctor — grade and fix a finished prompt

⚪All levels Paste a prompt you already wrote. The Prompt Doctor scores it against the prompting practices that matter for Claude, flags exactly what's weak, links each finding to the page that fixes it, and hands you an improved scaffold to copy. It runs 100% in your browser — your prompt is never sent anywhere. The checks are plain heuristics, not an AI call: fast, free, and transparent. PROMPT DOCTORdiagnostic console · v1 · local-only Paste a prompt — analyzed in your browser, nothing transmitted.weak samplestrong sample Heuristics, not an oracle — they catch the common failure modes, not every nuance. Use the linked pages to go deeper, and always test with a real eval.

Each rule maps to a deeper page on this site: Check Why it matters Enough to act on One-line prompts force Claude to guess your intent. Sets a role "You are a senior X" sharply focuses tone and judgment — see Roles. States one clear task A leading imperative verb ("Summarize…", "Extract…") removes ambiguity. Specifies output shape Without a format you get whatever length and structure Claude picks — see Structured Output. Shows an example For pattern tasks, one input→output pair beats paragraphs of description — see Few-Shot. Structured when long Long prompts need XML tags so Claude can tell instructions from content. Guards against made-up answers When you supply source text, tell Claude to use only it — the core defense against hallucination. Avoids vague qualifiers "good", "engaging", "professional" aren't measurable. Replace them with testable criteria. :::tip From score to ship A high score isn't the goal — a reliable result is. Treat the scaffold as a starting point, then prove it with a real eval before you depend on the output. :::

Prompting Basics — the five moves that fix most prompts Prompting Claude — what's specific to Claude The Pattern Library — copy-paste prompt patterns Prompt Builder — build a structured prompt from scratch

🟢Beginner Paste any prompt or document and get a quick token estimate — so you can budget context window and cost before you hit the API. All client-side; nothing leaves your browser. Paste some text here to see roughly how many tokens it is. 12words 58characters ~15–16estimated tokens ≈ <1% of a 200K-token context window A rough feel only (~chars ÷ 4, or words × 1.33). Token counts are model-specific — never use another model's tokenizer. For exact numbers use Anthropic's token-counting endpoint. :::tip Why tokens matter Context limits and pricing are both measured in tokens (≈ ¾ of a word). Trimming what you send cuts both cost and latency. Deep dive in Tokens, Context & Pricing. :::

🟡Intermediate Let's turn a fresh checkout into a Claude Code setup that knows your project and respects your rules — in about 20 minutes. We'll string together the core features with the rationale for each.
documents.s: Customize Claude Code for a Real Repo

documents.t: Run /init to draft a CLAUDE.md, then edit it down to what's true: stack, how to run/test/lint, real conventions, and guardrails ("run tests before done", "don't touch /generated"). Why: it's the highest-leverage customization — Claude reads it every session. Grab a starter from CLAUDE.md Templates.

Add a .claude/settings.json (reference) that pre-allows safe, repetitive commands and denies the dangerous: { "permissions": { "allow": ["Read", "Bash(npm run test:*)", "Bash(npm run lint)", "Bash(git diff:*)"], "ask": ["Write", "Bash(npm install:*)"], "deny": ["Read(./.env)", "Bash(git push --force:*)"] } } Why: fewer interruptions on safe actions, hard stops on risky ones. See Permissions.

Auto-format after every edit (hooks): { "hooks": { "PostToolUse": [ { "matcher": "Edit|Write", "hooks": [ { "type": "command", "command": "npx prettier --write \"$CLAUDE_FILE_PATH\" 2>/dev/null || true" } ] } ] } } Why: consistent formatting, guaranteed — not "please remember."
Drop the /commit recipe from the Slash Command Library into .claude/commands/. Why: one word for a repeatable workflow.
Give a real goal in Plan Mode, review the plan, then let it execute. Why: build trust by separating thinking from doing.
documents.s: Step 5 — Use Plan Mode for the first real task

documents.t: New session → Claude references your conventions unprompted (CLAUDE.md works). Editing a file → it gets formatted (hook works). A risky command → it asks or refuses (permissions work). /commit → a clean Conventional Commit message (command works).
Write Your First Skill Hooks & settings.json Recipes Coding & Software Development

🔴Advanced ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. MCP SDK APIs and config evolve — confirm against the official MCP docs and the Claude Code MCP docs. Confirm against the official source. Let's expose a custom tool to Claude by building a tiny MCP server and connecting it. We'll keep it minimal so the wiring is clear — then you swap in your real logic.
documents.s: Build & Wire Your First MCP Server

documents.t: A stdio server with one tool, word_count, that Claude can call. Same pattern scales to "query my DB", "open a ticket", etc.

server.py (Python; a TypeScript version is in MCP scaffolds): from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP mcp = FastMCP("text-tools") @mcp.tool() def word_count(text: str) -> int: """Count the words in a piece of text.""" return len(text.split()) if __name__ == "__main__": mcp.run() # stdio transport
Add to .mcp.json at your repo root: { "mcpServers": { "text-tools": { "command": "python", "args": ["server.py"] } } }

Start Claude Code in the repo. Ask: "Use the text-tools server to count the words in: 'the quick brown fox'." Claude should call word_count and report 4. If it can't see the tool, check the server starts cleanly on its own and the .mcp.json path is right.
Replace word_count with your actual capability — a DB query, an internal API call, a file operation. Add input validation and return errors as results.

:::warning A server is code + access Least privilege — only the data/actions it needs (Securing Agents). Validate inputs the model sends. Untrusted data it returns can carry prompt injection. Review any third-party server before connecting it. :::
MCP Servers in Claude Code MCP Config & Server Scaffolds Tool Use / Function Calling
🟡Intermediate A toy API call is one line. A production call handles errors, streams output, watches cost, and keeps secrets safe. Let's build that, step by step.
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..." # never in source control Keep the model ID in config, not scattered literals, so migration is trivial (why). Pick it deliberately — Choosing a Model.

Python TypeScript import os, time, random, anthropic client = anthropic.Anthropic() MODEL = os.environ.get("CLAUDE_MODEL", "claude-sonnet-5") def ask_stream(prompt, system=None, max_tokens=1024): for attempt in range(5): try: with client.messages.stream( model=MODEL, max_tokens=max_tokens, system=system or anthropic.NOT_GIVEN, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}], ) as stream: for text in stream.text_stream: print(text, end="", flush=True) final = stream.get_final_message() print() usage = final.usage print(f"\n[tokens in/out: {usage.input_tokens}/{usage.output_tokens}]") return final except (anthropic.RateLimitError, anthropic.APIStatusError): if attempt == 4: raise time.sleep(min(2 ** attempt + random.random(), 30)) import Anthropic from "@anthropic-ai/sdk"; const client = new Anthropic(); const MODEL = process.env.CLAUDE_MODEL ?? "claude-sonnet-5"; export async function askStream(prompt: string, system?: string, maxTokens = 1024) { for (let attempt = 0; attempt < 5; attempt++) { try { const stream = client.messages.stream({ model: MODEL, max_tokens: maxTokens, system, messages: [{ role: "user", content: prompt }] }); for await (const e of stream) if (e.type === "content_block_delta") process.stdout.write(e.delta.text ?? ""); const final = await stream.finalMessage(); console.error(`\n[tokens in/out: ${final.usage.input_tokens}/${final.usage.output_tokens}]`); return final; } catch (e: any) { if (attempt === 4 || ![429, 500, 529].includes(e?.status)) throw e; await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, Math.min(2 ** attempt * 1000, 30000))); } } }

Log token usage (above) so you can see what each call costs. Right-size max_tokens and the model; cap input with focused prompts. For repeated stable prefixes, add prompt caching. See Tokens & Pricing and Cost & Latency.
Retry transient errors (429/5xx) with backoff (above); don't retry 400s. Handle refusals gracefully. Set a timeout and a cost/iteration budget for anything agentic.
Run it: you should see streamed output, a token-usage line, and graceful behavior if you force an error (e.g. a bad key → clean message, not a crash).
Errors, Rate Limits & Reliability Production API Snippets Building Agents on the API

🟡Intermediate ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. Skill layout and discovery can change — confirm against the official Skills docs. Confirm against the official source. Let's build a working Skill from scratch and prove it activates. We'll make a small "changelog entry" skill — generic and reusable.
mkdir -p .claude/skills/changelog-entry (Use ~/.claude/skills/… for a personal skill across all projects.)

.claude/skills/changelog-entry/SKILL.md: --- name: changelog-entry description: Use when the user wants to turn recent git commits into a Keep a Changelog entry. --- # Changelog Entry When asked for a changelog entry: 1. Run `git log --oneline -20` to see recent commits. 2. Group them into Added / Changed / Fixed / Removed (Keep a Changelog style). 3. Write concise, user-facing bullets (not raw commit messages). 4. Output only the formatted entry. The description is the trigger — write it as "Use when…" so Claude loads it at the right time.
Skills can ship scripts. Add scripts/recent.sh and reference it from SKILL.md if you want deterministic data gathering: #!/usr/bin/env bash git log --oneline -20

Start a session and say: "Draft a changelog entry for recent work." Claude should recognize the intent, load the skill, and follow its steps. If it doesn't activate, your description probably isn't specific enough about when to use it — sharpen it.
Bundle it (with others) into a plugin so your team installs it in one step — or contribute it to AILmanac's skill packs.
Vague description → never triggers (or triggers always). Be specific. Too much in one skill → keep it one clear job. Secrets in a shared skill → never; see Reviewing Third-Party Code.
Skills: On-Demand Expertise SKILL.md Templates Build & Wire Your First MCP Server
🔴Advanced Big tasks go better when you split them across focused subagents instead of cramming everything into one context. Let's design a research → implement → review pipeline.
Each subagent has its own context and a tailored toolset — and only the result flows back to the main session, keeping it clean.

Via the /agents interface, define three, each with a tight description (so the main agent delegates correctly) and scoped tools: researcher — read/search only. Maps the relevant code and returns findings. implementer — can edit files and run tests; gets the researcher's findings as input. reviewer — read-only, adversarial: looks for bugs, missing cases, and convention violations.

The main session passes each stage's output to the next: research → implement (using the research) → review (of the implementation). Add a review gate: if the reviewer finds issues, loop back to the implementer before finishing.

:::warning Parallel/multi-agent isn't free Sequential dependencies (implement needs research) stay sequential — don't fan out where order matters. Shared file writes can conflict — isolate with git worktrees or serialize. For small tasks, the coordination overhead exceeds the benefit. Use this for sizeable, decomposable work. :::
documents.s: Step 3 — Know when NOT to do this

documents.t: A good multi-agent run shows: a focused main context (heavy reading happened in the researcher), an implementation that reflects the research, and a review that actually caught something (or credibly signed off). If the reviewer is a rubber stamp, make its prompt adversarial ("try to find what's wrong").
The same pattern, programmatically, is Building Agents on the API and product surfaces like Cowork & Agent Teams.
Subagents & Parallel Agents Git Worktrees Building Agents on the API

🔴Advanced ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-20. Headless flags and CI integration details evolve — confirm against the official Claude Code / Agent SDK docs. Confirm against the official source. A classic high-value automation: have Claude review every pull request and post its findings as a comment — running headless in CI. Here's the shape, with the guardrails that keep it safe.
documents.s: GitHub Action that Reviews Every PR

documents.t: On each PR: check out the diff, ask Claude to review it for bugs/edge-cases/convention issues, and post a comment. Humans still decide; Claude just gives a fast first pass.

name: Claude PR review on: pull_request permissions: contents: read pull-requests: write # to comment — NOT write to code jobs: review: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 with: { fetch-depth: 0 } - name: Review the diff env: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }} run: | git diff origin/${{ github.base_ref }}...HEAD > /tmp/diff.patch claude -p "Review this diff for correctness bugs, missing edge cases, and security issues. Report ONLY high-confidence findings as a Markdown checklist with file:line. Diff:" < /tmp/diff.patch > /tmp/review.md # then post /tmp/review.md as a PR comment (e.g. with the gh CLI or an action) (Exact headless invocation may differ — see the docs. The principle is: feed the diff, capture Markdown, post it.)

:::warning Least privilege in CI Comment only. Grant pull-requests: write, not contents: write — the bot shouldn't push code. Scope the token; never expose deploy/secret access to a job that reads untrusted PR content. Treat PR content as untrusted — it can carry prompt injection; don't let the job take consequential actions. Cap cost — large diffs cost tokens; consider reviewing changed files only. :::
Ask for high-confidence findings only — a wall of nitpicks gets ignored. Keep it as a first pass, with humans making the merge call.
Headless Mode & the Agent SDK Hardening Autonomous Runs Coding & Software Development

🟡Intermediate ✅ Last verified: 2026-07-01. Deprecation schedules and migration details come from Anthropic and change — always confirm dates and replacements in the official docs before migrating. Confirm against the official source. Models get retired, parameters get renamed, behaviors shift. This page tracks breaking changes so upgrades don't surprise you. If you build on the API, skim it periodically.
A pinned model ID will eventually be deprecated and then removed. Code that hard-codes it breaks. A little hygiene prevents 2am surprises.

Read model IDs from config, never scatter literals across the codebase — then migrating is a one-line change. Watch announcements for deprecation dates and recommended replacements. Re-run your evals when you switch models — behavior can shift even on a "newer, better" model. Test before the deadline, not after removal.

Change Example What to do Model retired An older model ID stops working Move to the recommended successor; re-eval Parameter superseded A reasoning/budget knob replaced by effort Update calls; see Thinking & Effort Default shift A new default model/behavior Pin intentionally; verify your prompts still work Endpoint/SDK update New SDK major version Read the migration guide; bump deliberately
The authoritative schedule lives on the official model deprecations page — confirm dates there before you migrate. Anthropic gives at least 60 days' notice before retiring a publicly released model.

Model ID Deprecated Retires Move to claude-opus-4-1-20250805 (Opus 4.1) June 5, 2026 August 5, 2026 claude-opus-4-8 Requests to a model past its retirement date fail — if you pin Opus 4.1, migrate before the deadline.
claude-opus-4-20250514 (Opus 4) and claude-sonnet-4-20250514 (Sonnet 4) were retired June 15, 2026. Calls to those IDs now error — move to claude-opus-4-8 and claude-sonnet-5 respectively.

On Claude Opus 4.7 and later (including Opus 4.8), the sampling knobs temperature, top_p, and top_k are deprecated: setting any of them to a non-default value returns a 400 error. Omit them and steer behavior with prompting and Thinking & Effort instead. Always confirm your model IDs against the Current Models & Pricing table; if you're on an older ID, plan a migration.
Errors, Rate Limits & Reliability Current Models & Pricing Evaluating AI Quality (Evals)

AILmanac's one promise is to be honest about freshness. Most docs sites say they're current; this page proves it. Every page below is scored against its last verification date — computed against your clock, right now, so this dashboard can never quietly go stale itself. :::tip This is the easiest way to contribute See a page marked 🔴 Stale or ⚪ Unverified? That's an open invitation. Click Verify, check the page against its official source, and add (or bump) its <VerifyNote lastVerified="YYYY-MM-DD" />. One date is a complete, welcome contribution — see Contribute in 10 Minutes. :::

🟢 Fresh — verified within the last 90 days. 🟡 Aging — verified 90–180 days ago; worth a glance. 🔴 Stale — verified over 180 days ago; likely drifted from the source. ⚪ Unverified — no <VerifyNote> yet. Volatile pages (model names, prices, limits, UI labels) should always carry one. The data is rebuilt from the docs on every site build by scripts/build-freshness.mjs, which reads each page's most recent <VerifyNote lastVerified="…"> stamp. Nothing to maintain by hand — add a stamp to a page and it shows up here automatically.

⚪All levels This is the one page in AILmanac with a hard freshness guarantee. Every model fact elsewhere on the site links here instead of hard-coding a name or number, so there's a single place to keep current.

✅ Last verified: 2026-07-01 · Official models & pricing Model API model ID Tier Best for Claude Opus 4.8 claude-opus-4-8 Opus — most capable The hardest reasoning, long-horizon agents, and complex coding where quality matters more than cost. Claude Sonnet 5 claude-sonnet-5 Sonnet — balanced The default workhorse and Claude Code's default model: the best combination of speed and intelligence, with a 1M-token context window. Start here for most work. (Supersedes Claude Sonnet 4.6, now a legacy model.) Claude Haiku 4.5 claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 Haiku — fastest & cheapest High-volume, latency-sensitive tasks: classification, extraction, routing, simple chat, and cheap sub-agents. Fable 5 claude-fable-5 Latest generation Anthropic's most capable widely released model, for the most demanding reasoning and long-horizon agentic work. Generally available since June 2026. Model IDs are exact; tiers and 'best for' are guidance. Pricing, context-window sizes and rate limits change frequently and are intentionally NOT listed here — always read them on the official page above. This file is the single source of truth: never hard-code model facts in prose, link to this table instead.

They change too often to copy safely — a stale price is worse than no price. We list the identity of each model (name, API ID, what it's for) and send you to the official page for pricing, context windows, and rate limits, which is always authoritative.
documents.s: Why pricing and context limits aren't in the table

documents.t: The table is rendered from a single data file (data/models.json) carrying a "last verified" date. Updating it is a one-line change — a perfect first contribution. In a later phase, automation will diff this file against the official models endpoint and open a pull request when something drifts — but a human always reviews before it ships. (See How Our Automation Works.)

A quick rule of thumb (full guidance in Choosing a Model): Start with Sonnet. Best balance of quality, speed, and cost for most work. Reach for Opus when quality on a hard problem matters more than cost. Drop to Haiku for high-volume, latency-sensitive, or simple tasks.

⚪All levels Changes to AILmanac itself — structure, features, and major content drops. (For changes to Claude, see This Month in Claude.) Format follows Keep a Changelog.

Full Claude Code and Claude API sections (every page written). AI Foundations, Prompting, Claude.ai & Apps, Playbooks, Templates, and Security sections completed. Interactive widgets: Prompt Builder, Model Picker, Token Estimator. 7 skill packs (/skills) for different user types. Branded Open Graph social card; premium theme + animated homepage. Freshness system: data/models.json source of truth + <VerifyNote> stamps.
Premium visual redesign (typography, palette, custom logo).
This is a community project — see who contributed in the repo. Want your change logged here? Mention it in your PR (Contribute). Subscribe to changes by watching the GitHub repo.

⚪All levels ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-24. This page is a rolling, human-curated digest. For the authoritative, complete list of changes, always see the official Anthropic docs and the Claude Code changelog. Confirm against the official source. A plain-English digest of what's changed recently across Claude — so you can skim "what's new and what it means for me" without reading every changelog. Newest first. :::info How this page stays current Entries are added by the community and by an automated watcher that flags upstream changes for a human to write up. Each entry links its official source. See something missing? Add it. :::
Each update is tagged by surface — 🗣️ Apps (chat/voice/mobile), 🛠️ Claude Code, 🔌 API, 🧠 Models — and answers "what it means for you."

🧠 Models — Claude Sonnet 5 shipped June 30, 2026 and is now the default model in Claude Code, with a native 1M-token context window and introductory pricing through August 31, 2026. It's the new balanced "start here" Sonnet tier, superseding Claude Sonnet 4.6 (now a legacy model). What it means: if you pin a model, migrate to claude-sonnet-5; and always check the Current Models & Pricing table for exact IDs and live pricing before relying on a number from a blog post. ✅ Last verified: 2026-07-01. API ID claude-sonnet-5; Sonnet tier; 1M-token context; 128k max output; Jan 2026 knowledge cutoff. Introductory pricing of $2 / $10 per MTok applies through Aug 31, 2026 (standard $3 / $15). Now the Claude Code default per the changelog (v2.1.197, June 30 2026). Confirm against the official source. 🛠️ Claude Code — Dynamic workflows are in research preview: ask for one (or set /effort ultracode) and Claude writes a JavaScript script that orchestrates tens to hundreds of subagents in the background — for codebase-wide audits, large migrations, and cross-checked research. Run /deep-research to see the bundled one, or /workflows to watch runs. What it means: you can now take on fleet-scale tasks one conversation could never coordinate, with the orchestration saved as a rerunnable command. See Dynamic Workflows & ultracode. ✅ Last verified: 2026-06-24. Requires Claude Code v2.1.154+; available on paid plans, the API, Bedrock, Vertex, and Foundry. Confirm against the official source. 🧠 Models — A new top-tier model, Claude Fable 5, shipped in June 2026 (Claude Code v2.1.170+). What it means: check the Current Models & Pricing table for the live lineup and exact IDs before pinning a model — never hard-code model facts from a blog post. 🛠️ Claude Code — Subagents can now spawn their own subagents (up to 5 levels deep), and /code-review --fix applies review findings to your working tree. What it means: delegation composes deeper, and review can close the loop instead of just reporting. Cross-check the official changelog for exact behavior. 🔌 API — Capabilities like structured output, extended thinking, and prompt caching evolve. What it means: re-check the API section before relying on exact parameters.
This is a digest, not a complete log. For everything: Anthropic docs & announcements Claude Code changelog Deprecation & Migration Watch
